content.title: GitHub - disler/claude-code-hooks-mastery: Master Claude Code Hooks · GitHub
Claude Code hooks explained: the missing layer between prompts and production
GitHub - karanb192/claude-code-hooks: 🪝 A growing collection of useful Claude Code hooks. Copy, paste, customize.
Claude Code Hooks: Complete Guide to All 12 Lifecycle Events
Claude Code Hooks: Automate Your AI Coding Workflow
Steering Claude Code: skills, hooks, subagents and more | Claude by Anthropic
Claude Code Hooks Complete Guide — Automate and Enforce Rules Reliably - SmartScope
Claude Code Hooks: The Feature You’re Ignoring While Babysitting Your AI | by Lakshmi Narasimhan | Medium

results:
Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Claude Code hooks:

## What Are Claude Code Hooks?

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Think of hooks like git hooks, but for your AI agent. You define handlers - shell commands, LLM prompts, or even sub-agents - that fire automatically at specific points during Claude Code's execution. Before a tool runs, after a file gets edited, when the agent decides to stop, when a session starts.

## Hook Types

There are several types of hooks: command, HTTP, mcp_tool, prompt, and agent. Each serves different purposes:

- Command hooks (type: "command"): run a shell command. Your script receives the event's JSON input on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout.
- HTTP hooks (type: "http"): send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL. The endpoint communicates results back through the response body using the same JSON output format as command hooks.
- Prompt hooks (type: "prompt"): send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation. The model returns a yes/no decision as JSON.

## Key Advantages

The critical difference from prompt-based rules is that hooks are guaranteed to execute. If you tell Claude Code in your CLAUDE.md not to modify .env files, it will probably listen. If you set up a PreToolUse hook that blocks writes to .env files, it will always block them.

## Common Use Cases

Popular applications include automatic code formatting every time Claude Code writes or edits a file via a PostToolUse hook, eliminating the need to manually run formatters after every change, and using the Stop event to force Claude to keep working if verification fails by combining it with a prompt hook that evaluates whether all requested tasks were completed.

The page serves as a placeholder domain owned by IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority). Its primary purpose is to provide a safe, reserved namespace for developers and technical writers to reference in instructional materials without risk of interfering with actual live websites.

The brief notice encourages users to avoid deploying this domain in production environments, instead directing interested parties to IANA's documentation for additional context about example domains and their appropriate usage guidelines.

content.title: GitHub - disler/claude-code-hooks-mastery: Master Claude Code Hooks · GitHub
Claude Code hooks explained: the missing layer between prompts and production
GitHub - karanb192/claude-code-hooks: 🪝 A growing collection of useful Claude Code hooks. Copy, paste, customize.
Claude Code Hooks: Complete Guide to All 12 Lifecycle Events
Claude Code Hooks: Automate Your AI Coding Workflow
Steering Claude Code: skills, hooks, subagents and more | Claude by Anthropic
Claude Code Hooks Complete Guide — Automate and Enforce Rules Reliably - SmartScope
Claude Code Hooks: The Feature You’re Ignoring While Babysitting Your AI | by Lakshmi Narasimhan | Medium

results:
I found comprehensive information about Claude Code hooks. Here's what I found:

## Overview

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session.

## Hook Types

There are several types of hooks: command, HTTP, mcp_tool, prompt, and agent. Specifically:
- Command hooks run a shell command, where your script receives the event's JSON input on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout.
- HTTP hooks send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL, with the endpoint communicating results back through the response body.
- Prompt hooks send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation, with the model returning a yes/no decision as JSON.
- Agent hooks spawn a subagent that can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to verify conditions before returning a decision.

## Key Benefits

Hooks inject deterministic processing into Claude Code's lifecycle. Unlike prompt instructions, which are requests that Claude may interpret flexibly, hooks execute the same way every time.

The critical difference from prompt-based rules is that hooks are guaranteed to execute. If you set up a PreToolUse hook that blocks writes to .env files, it will always block them.

## Common Use Cases

Some popular applications include:
- Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically, eliminating formatting inconsistencies.
- Rather than giving Claude Code blanket permission and hoping for the best, you set up a PreToolUse hook that inspects every bash command before it runs.
- The Stop event fires whenever Claude finishes responding, and if your hook returns a blocking signal, Claude is forced to keep working. Combine this with a prompt hook to get an AI agent that verifies its own work before stopping.

You can find the full documentation at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
